← Bioregional Organizations

Guayabillas DAO

Case Study
Y
background_section
## Background Guayabillas de Pacto sits in the Chocó-Andean Biodiversity Region, a globally important biodiversity hotspot facing deforestation, degradation from unsustainable agriculture, and limited governance capacity at local levels. ### Origins and Network Traditional conservation models often rely on external organizations managing funds and making decisions for communities, creating dependency and limiting local agency. The Guayabillas DAO project emerged to address core challenges: how can communities gain real governance power over funding and priorities? How can individual conservation actions be connected to collective regenerative work? How can innovative financing mechanisms support community-led regeneration while ensuring transparency and accountability? Through a pioneering approach that combines participatory design, blockchain-based governance, and novel funding mechanisms like NFTree conservation payments, the project demonstrates how local communities can manage their own regenerative work with both autonomy and transparency—proving that community-led conservation can be both effective and sustainable.
Sources
33314415-10e1-814e-9f0a-d8e0753f96d5
patterns
Storying_Place|Governance_Bodies|Commons_Governance_Protocols|Participatory_Planning_and_Strategies|Geographic_and_Ecological_Mapping|Vegetation_Planting|Results_Based_Mecanisms|Grants_Programs|Knowledge_Artefacts
Place
Guayabillas, Bolívar, Colombia
place
Guayabillas de Pacto, Bolívar, Cauca, Colombia
activities
Storying Guayabillas|DAO Design|NFTree Conservation Payments|Landscape Planning and Pattern Language|Project Development
description
Pioneers a regenerative governance and financing model using blockchain technology for community-led conservation and restoration in the CABR biodiversity hotspot, combining participatory design, nested governance structures, and innovative funding mechanisms.
vision_legacy_section
## Vision and Legacy The DAO is operational under local stewardship. Training stresses both off-chain and on-chain practice, with smartphones and WhatsApp lowering technical barriers and deliberate outreach to women, elders, and youth. The pilot is meant to scale across neighbouring communities and parishes—nested governance that can link smallholder practice to wider ecological and finance systems.
bioregional_context
The CABR (Chocó-Andean biodiversity region) is a globally important biodiversity hotspot facing deforestation, degradation from unsustainable agriculture, and limited governance capacity at local levels, requiring new approaches to conservation financing and community empowerment.
category
Bioregional Organizer
